speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Leopold Zylberman. This is tape six. [? Lucek, ?] could you tell me what your first impressions of Shanghai were when you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I heard about Shanghai before I came. But the impression was, it was stinking hot. And I saw people dressed, you see, and walking around with terry towels, you see, and wiping the sweat from the-- you see taking off the hats, et cetera. You know, that means we came out to the [NON-ENGLISH]. And you see, there are a couple of nice buildings, some house, and the Palace Hotel. It looked like a big town. And I can't remember where they took us. But anyway, a friend and I, two married couples, we read an advertisement of a-- two rooms to stay in the Western District.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you read it? What language was it in?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was English. There was an English newspaper. There was a German newspaper, edited by Germany, Germans. And there was another German newspaper edited by the German Jews. And there were the two Russian papers. And we knew everything, what was going on. There was a radio station in German, a radio station in Russian, a radio station in England then. Well, in the meantime, over the period after we came to Shanghai, we kept on learning. So we had an idea about Russian and about English, and about German, which was sort of familiar with Jews, and especially when I speak, Hebrews know Hebrew. And Yiddish is a mixture of Polish, German and Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I came across a Hebrew word, when I was translating from Yiddish to German, you see, trying to talk German, I came across a Hebrew word. So I stopped, and I tried to explain. So you-- you mean this. You see, for instance I say that the we were sailing the sea. So I knew that the sea in Hebrew is yam. You see the rest was German. Sea was yam. So I say, what do you mean the-- plenty of water or something?

speaker: interviewee
text: You then can [NON-ENGLISH] is the sea. So I knew by that, I learned that a lot of German. You see, that means I'm quite conversant. You know, I have not a big wealth, but a few words for the everyday living. And we--

speaker: interviewer
text: You read this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we sort of found accommodation, you know, oh-- and two friends who came. You see, a month ago, they started selling textiles. And they had me as a bookkeeper. And there was a few bob from the Jewish Welfare Society. And we started, we tried to make this mouth water, you see, that was mouthwash, you know, which we were manufacturing in Warsaw. And I had the recipe. And this was a Russian formula, you know that means that was a finished Russian formula. It was popular with their Russians. And there were few Russian chemists out the refugees from the First World War. And somehow we were taken care of by the Russian Jews, who hated the German Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just because the German Jews hated the Russian Jews. And they thought about themselves of something better. You see, there's always be-- there was the [NON-ENGLISH], you know, there was something. It was category, and those people you know, they were making big money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it seem-- did it seem odd to you--

speaker: interviewee
text: They had warehouses full of merchandise, and they were helping us. Now the German Jews who had money and brains, they established themselves in a shop, or in some manufacturing. The Russian-- let me see-- one who didn't have, you see, the business brains or something like this, there were a lot of people walking around the offices, and selling pens, pencils, stationery, suit lengths. And they were their boss. You know, there was a fellow, with a big-- who had money, and had a big store of textiles. So we went cutting 3 and a 1/4 yards. And people used to go down from one office to the other selling them. And those people-- you know, let me see, started he gave us a [NON-ENGLISH], a gold watch. So we got two suit lengths, and went out to the city to the offices and sold it. And so he came back for another. That's-- that's how they may a living. And these two friends of mine, they started the same kind of business. And I was the bookkeeper. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you living at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were living in the Western District, with-- together with the other fellow, a druggist, you see that means who had a drugstore in Warsaw, where he had a few bob, and we were in partnership to do this antiseptic.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you describe the-- your living conditions in Shanghai in the Western District?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very good. That means we were in a house owned by Chinese that actually they were refugees from Canton or something. It was a beautiful house, and we had a big bathroom to share. You see, we live in [NON-ENGLISH], and they were a family. And they had a-- it was kind of a palace. And it sounded very cheap.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Jewish community in Shanghai form committees, clubs, social events.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That means that the Russian Jews had a club. And we were welcome at the club. And they were helping us, you see, that means with the hospital, with everything. But this didn't last very long, you see. Because when Pearl Harbor started the Japanese took over everything. You see, that means we traveled by tram to-- to [NON-ENGLISH], to Central Shanghai, to take another tram to Hongkew, to the sort of Japanese city. Because where the poor gentleman refugees live with the best of Jews to get fixed. And when we came to the Soochow Creek, of Huangpu River. You see, the Japanese were coming up. And we saw for the first time a Japanese flag in the Huangpu River when we left out from Nanking road, you see on the bend and something was strong. And the bridge was closed, you see, bridge over the Soochow Creek. And a few minutes later, transport trucks with soldiers were crossing the bridge. And we couldn't get. So we took the shoes, to go back to the-- go back to the home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Lola with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, all the time. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on the way back, we had to walk to change trains, the one from international settlement to the French Concession. Because there was some [INAUDIBLE]. And already in front of every building was a Japanese guard, you see. And the big trouble that we-- we had a few of dollars. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the dollar was-- the exchange rate was 40 odd say Shanghai dollars. And when Japanese occupied, it fell to 15. Somehow money lost the few bob we got left was-- lost two thirds of its value. And we didn't know what to do. And we gave up the manufacturing. You see, that means mouthwash manufacturing. I still have the prints.

speaker: interviewer
text: What prints? What are you talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the packing. You see, I had printed, you see, boxes and labels to put on the bottles. There were some-- some were in my daughter's basement. It's not important. And then we moved over to a flat in the French Concessions, and Avenue [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we didn't have any money, and our partners, we sold the partnership, and he was paying for me. You know, and we in a flat. It was a three-room flat. And we got the servant's flat, which was sort of 6-foot wide, you see, and 6 foot-- 8 foot long, you see. So the bed and room to pass through, and just hanging this-- and it was the servant room. So there was a Russian woman, you see, with a little boy. And then was a couple with the little boy in one room, and another couple in the second room. And we looked what we're going to do. And you see, we started traveling second class. Because there were two classes, first and second class on the trains. And eventually, you see, people were running out of money. And they came. And they didn't want to go to Hongkew to those warehouses and three-story beds. You see, and with the help of the Jews, now the Jewish club got a lease of the American club. You know, which was beautifully equipped you see, with 10-pin bowling, and also they were not automatic. Because the Chinese were standing and putting back the things-- and-- the pins. And the old club, they gave the grand floor to the Shiva. The first floor for living quarters for the Shiva [NON-ENGLISH]. And the second floor, there was a few rooms. So they put up curtains. And in every room were three couples, and in one room was 10 bunch of boys. And I was very nice, just very lucky. At the end of the corridor was a window. So I rustled up a couple of sheets of [NON-ENGLISH], you know, and I made myself a room from the end of the corridor.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing there, if this was a place for the Shiva.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- that was you see that everything stops, all sort of business stops. In the meantime, we didn't have money to pay for the servant room.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the French Quarter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And that means the Jewish club got the premises of the marine club, and all old Jewish club, and [NON-ENGLISH] was divided. You see? That means sheets across strings, are all made into curtains. And you see the four couples to a room, five couples. You see, there was this. And I had a room for myself. Because you see the end of corridor, I don't remember how, but you see people said that I was-- that was a genius move that I picked up the [NON-ENGLISH] and made myself a room. You see, so that was one bed standing. And we rustled up, you see, that means the charitable rich Jewish community, that would get a piece of furniture, a chair or something like this. And we got sort of a flower hibachi to cook the stuff, to cook room.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said the-- you said the rich Jewish people helped you out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Russian Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the Russians, and the Russians with money.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, they were all-- they all had-- you see, they were a very [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] close-knit community. And there was the rabbi, and Mr. Klebbenof, and Mr. [INAUDIBLE]. And you see that there was a couple of Polish Jews among them. But you see, they were going as a Russian. And when we moved and we were getting from the committee, you see, from the-- they organized. And at that time, they outlaid the money to give us the help. And the money was put into their account in America. That's what I found out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you money was put into their account in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, they-- they gave us the money, which was supposed to come from Switzerland and America. So that's the way they transferred the money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you saying that the-- the charity--

speaker: interviewee
text: They made a good business. But for us, it was terrific. Because we lived there, and we sort of cooked with little briquettes or something. But we could go to the real Jewish club, the American club. And during the day, they were in business, and we played the billiard tournament. And we played 10-pin bowling.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we sort of had a ball. We didn't have any money. But somehow-- and then in-- you see, but we lived outside. There was no ghetto yet. You know? So business started again slowly. And-- Did the Japanese put any restrictions on the Jewish community after that--

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, there was no Japanese.

speaker: interviewer
text: I thought you said the Japanese came down the river.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Let me which I said, at that time you see we still lived outside the ghetto. You know, and it was going-- the French Concession, without the French people who went to the-- French citizens to the camps, and without English citizens. But life was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is this after the beginning of the Pacific War?

speaker: interviewee
text: The--

speaker: interviewer
text: When the Japanese entered the war, is this after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The only thing was that business was bad, you see, because the dollar fall down. And-- and-- but we didn't change our way of life, except you see some people still had enough money to keep a room in a flat. And some boys were-- you see a few in a room. And you see sort of sitting burning, the-- burning the mosquito incense, so to run away the mosquitoes. And-- and meeting in a cafe. There was an [NON-ENGLISH], a cafe that everybody met. Because you see, it was cold, to get warm. We warmed up and have a cup of tea. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go hungry all at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who supplied the food for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there was a market, and there were shops. And I was still, you see, that means this two partners. They started back the business. You know, and I had my job. I had my support of the thing. And we didn't need much.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened next? In the meantime, I was-- I got sick. But I still was going to work, you see, from the club into the downtown, the city. And-- and then I was lucky that, you see, that I was in the room. And there wasn't much hope for me. But just to say, you see, we were pushing through. You see, I had a friend who finished medicine in Switzerland. Was a doctor yet, but he gave me injections.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was wrong with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had TB. Be I was coughing, and et cetera. And then there was the proclamation to go to the restricted area for refugees. It was not a ghetto. Actually, it was a restricted area for alien refugees. And I used my card that I was a sick man, and got myself 3-month extension, and another 3-month extension, and another 3-month.

speaker: interviewer
text: Extension to do what?

speaker: interviewee
text: To stay back in, and think. So they liquidated the Melbourne, the all-Jewish club. And they built a warehouse in Hongkew, that means in the restricted area. And I got myself a room with a family.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the nationality of the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Russian-Jewish. You see, a woman with a few kids. And she was letting out two rooms to me and another party.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was-- this was outside Hongkew, outside the restricted areas.

speaker: interviewee
text: Outside, it was the French Concession.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Lola with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. All the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So while all the other Jews were told to go to the restricted area, because of your card, are you saying that you stayed out?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, some people found excuses, and quite a few people, you see, were outside. And one day that came the normal bloody extensions, and everybody goes to the ghetto. And one by one, you see, the Japanese civilians who came after the army, they had a list of accommodation of the refugees. So they got accommodation for themselves while the refugees went to the ghetto. And whatever it cost, you see that means while a fellow was moving, one got three days and one got seven days. And well, I got 24 hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: For what?

speaker: interviewee
text: For being [NON-ENGLISH] in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean-- when you say you got 24 hours, what do you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: To go to jail, to the bunker.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you-- what was the reason that you went to jail?

speaker: interviewee
text: That means I over-used the extension of being outside. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened, you see the meantime, you see we had a-- an attic room, with a Jewish family. Actually they were Polish Jews who lived in Germany. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their Name

speaker: interviewee
text: Pytka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell it

speaker: interviewee
text: P-Y-T-K-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a made-to-measure tailor. And they were making good money, because they were making all the-- all the Japanese officers or something, you see, they used to find European tailor to make them suits by the time that they got to Siberia. And they gave us an attic room for very cheap. And we organized a-- a washbasin. And you see the lavatory, they was not so rich. And there was a portable, loose you see, and a big stink when they were coming early in the morning to clean.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for an or so, we had to close the windows until the smell goes. And so we lived there. This is before--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we're coming down to Mr. and Mrs. Pytka, and we were happy. We're happy. Not one moment did we think that the war would be lost. So funny, you see, that means we see-- with the blitz over London, and-- and friends and you know, that we did-- we were sure that the war would go, and we will be-- go back to Warsaw, or whatever it is. And in this ghetto, there was-- we have this attic room. And would put up a stove. And we bought a half a ton of coke. You see, that was a stove which was for warming and for cooking. You see that means there was the you see cast iron stove for the two upstairs. And we were cooking. And you see we needed to save fuel. We used to go to a cafe called Barcelona in the district. I was run by these industrious Viennese Jews, a couple who later came, and run a restaurant here. And you see, for the price of a cup of tea, you sit next to the combustion oven. And somehow, you're telling jokes. You see people telling. And everybody was somebody back at home. And you see, I got at the age of 21 or 22, I became an executive. They were telling such fantastic stories of how they lived, and how they sort of screwed the prima ballerinas of the ballet. And I was-- just, shut up. You see, that means I was a nobody. And I never took part in the conversation. You see, I was just a listener. And that was the time, we were in ghetto, you see that means we lived on the second floor above the Shiva. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I got the contact through Switzerland, because we were our friends, renting in Swiss [NON-ENGLISH]. And they got in contact with my oldest brother, who in the meantime could managed to go to Brazil. Because he sort of made his way through Italy and Barcelona.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I got a thousands Swiss francs. This is while you were in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the money get to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Through the bank, because Switzerland was neutral. And I got a thousand francs.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you do with this money?

speaker: interviewee
text: We felt-- you see let me-- with this money we had to go to the bank. And we had a big pack of Shanghai dollars. You see that was the Central Reserve Bank, all the dollars issued by the Nanjing, the Japanese puppet government. It was a big pack of money, and we were afraid to board the bus. And we walked all the way. You see, back, you see because we had to take a bus. And all the way back. This was-- no, this was before the ghetto. When we lived above the Shiva. You know. and we got this money. And we came back. And on the way back, we bought a pound of biscuits, and two pounds of lard, you know. And we cooked the lard in-- in this hibachi. And the lard tipped over. You see, and there was strife. And the floor started burning, but we just saved up the lard. And the money I spent on-- on medicines. That was shortly before the ghetto was formed.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're just going to take a break now to change tapes.
